choices.explanation: The passage explicitly states inflation 'benefited merchants and landowners... while devastating wage earners whose purchasing power fell' — different social classes experienced opposite effects from the same inflation.

choices.text: China received more silver than Europe from the Americas, which is why China experienced more severe inflation than Europe in the sixteenth century
choices.explanation: The passage says China had price stability while Europe had 400–600% inflation — the opposite of what this option claims. And the passage does not say China received more silver than Europe.

choices.text: American silver had no effect on Chinese prices because Ming China refused to accept foreign silver and used only copper cash internally
choices.explanation: The passage states Ming China's tax system required silver payments — Ming China actively sought and absorbed silver. The passage describes Chinese price changes (initial stability, then late Ming inflation) resulting from silver influx.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"The Kingdom of Dahomey in modern Benin became one of West Africa's most powerful states in the eighteenth century. Dahomey conducted regular slave raids on neighboring peoples and sold captives to European merchants at the port of Ouidah. Dahomey's king held a monopoly on the slave trade — no private individuals could sell enslaved people without royal authorization. Annual 'customs' (ceremonies) at Ouidah formally opened the trading season with elaborate ritual. By 1750, Dahomey was exporting 10,000–20,000 enslaved people annually."
— Adapted from a history of Dahomey and the Atlantic slave trade

questions.question: The Dahomey example described in the passage most directly illustrates which of the following about the Atlantic slave trade's relationship to African political structures?

choices.text: Some African states developed highly organized, state-controlled systems for supplying enslaved people to the Atlantic trade, using it as a source of state revenue and political power
choices.explanation: Dahomey's royal monopoly on slave trading, annual customs opening trading seasons, and 10,000–20,000 annual exports represent an institutionalized state commercial enterprise, not informal raiding. The slave trade was incorporated into Dahomey's state structure as a revenue and power source — demonstrating African states' active, organized participation in the Atlantic trade.

choices.text: The Atlantic slave trade strengthened all African states equally, making every kingdom in West Africa more powerful than before European contact
choices.explanation: The slave trade benefited some states (Dahomey, Asante) that were on the supplying side while devastating states and communities that were raided. The effects were unequal, not equally strengthening.

choices.text: Dahomey only sold enslaved people to European merchants under military coercion from European navies that blockaded the Ouidah harbor
choices.explanation: The passage describes Dahomey's active, institutionalized slave trading with elaborate ceremonies and royal monopoly — there is no mention of European coercion. Dahomey chose to trade enslaved people as a state economic strategy.

choices.text: Dahomey's slave trade was conducted entirely by private merchants without any involvement from the Dahomean state or royal family
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly describes the king's monopoly on the slave trade — private individuals could not sell without royal authorization. State control, not private enterprise, characterized Dahomey's participation.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"English colonization of the Atlantic seaboard followed a different model from Spanish colonization. While Spain focused on extracting mineral wealth from already-dense indigenous populations in Mexico and Peru, England colonized regions of lower indigenous population density, established settler colonies of permanent European families, and emphasized agricultural production (tobacco in Virginia, grain in New England). Indigenous people in English territories were largely displaced rather than incorporated as a labor force as they had been in Spanish colonies."
— Adapted from a comparison of European colonial models

questions.question: The contrast between English and Spanish colonial models described in the passage most directly illustrates which of the following differences in early modern European colonialism?

choices.text: Colonial strategies were shaped by the demographic and geographic conditions of colonized territories — extraction colonies where dense populations provided labor differed structurally from settler colonies that displaced sparse indigenous populations
choices.explanation: The passage attributes the different colonial models to different conditions: Spain found dense indigenous populations in Mexico/Peru available for labor extraction (encomienda/mita possible); England found lower-density populations in eastern North America where labor extraction was less viable, producing settler colonialism and indigenous displacement instead. The structure of colonialism was shaped by pre-existing conditions, not solely by the colonizers' preferences.

choices.text: England was a more humane colonial power than Spain because English colonists treated indigenous people with greater respect and avoided all violence
choices.explanation: The passage describes indigenous displacement by English colonizers — there is no claim of greater humaneness. Displacement and genocide accompanied English colonization; it was different in form (settler vs. extraction), not in humaneness.

choices.text: Spanish colonialism failed economically while English colonialism succeeded, because extraction colonies were inherently less profitable than settler colonies
choices.explanation: Spanish extraction colonies (especially Potosí silver mining) were enormously profitable — Spain extracted 181,000 tons of silver. Comparing profitability in terms of 'inherent' structural superiority doesn't reflect historical reality.

choices.text: England was unable to enslave indigenous populations due to legal prohibitions, so it adopted settler colonialism by default
choices.explanation: The passage attributes the difference to demographic conditions (lower indigenous population density), not to legal prohibitions on indigenous enslavement. English colonizers did enslave indigenous people in some contexts — legal prohibition was not the primary explanatory factor.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"Jesuit missionaries in China adopted a strategy of cultural accommodation: they dressed in Chinese scholar robes, learned classical Chinese, translated scientific texts, and presented Christianity as compatible with Confucian ethics. Jesuit mathematician Matteo Ricci gained access to the Ming court by offering European clocks, maps, and scientific instruments. The Jesuits debated whether Chinese ancestor rites — a central Confucian practice — were religious (requiring Christian condemnation) or merely civil custom (compatible with Christianity). The 'Chinese Rites Controversy' was eventually settled by the Pope in 1715, who condemned the rites — effectively ending Jesuit influence in China."
— Adapted from a history of Jesuit missions in Asia

questions.question: The Jesuit missionary strategy in China, as described in the passage, most directly illustrates which of the following approaches to cross-cultural interaction?

choices.text: Refusing all contact with non-Christian cultures and requiring immediate and complete conversion to European Christianity as a precondition for any interaction
choices.explanation: The passage describes the opposite — the Jesuits actively accommodated Chinese culture, dressed in scholar robes, learned classical Chinese, and presented Christianity as compatible with Confucianism. This was maximum cultural accommodation, not refusal of contact.

choices.text: The complete abandonment of Christian doctrine by Jesuit missionaries who converted to Confucianism after arriving in China
choices.explanation: The passage describes Jesuits presenting Christianity to the Chinese court, not abandoning it. Cultural accommodation meant presenting Christianity in culturally compatible ways, not renouncing it.

choices.text: Adapting the presentation of one's own cultural and religious system to align with local norms and intellectual frameworks to gain acceptance, at the cost of internal tensions about how far adaptation should go
choices.explanation: The Jesuits adopted Chinese dress, language, and framed Christianity in Confucian terms — cultural accommodation to gain access and acceptance. But the Chinese Rites Controversy shows the internal tension this created: if you accommodate too much (tolerating ancestor rites), you risk theological compromise; if you don't accommodate enough (condemning rites), you lose access. The Pope's 1715 condemnation ended accommodation and with it, Jesuit influence — showing the limits of selective adaptation.

choices.text: Proving that European scientific technology was sufficient on its own to convert Chinese officials to Christianity without any cultural adaptation needed
choices.explanation: The passage shows that scientific instruments opened doors (access to the Ming court) but the Jesuits also adopted extensive cultural accommodation (robes, language, Confucian framing) — technology alone was not sufficient for sustained religious influence.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"The 'Price Revolution' of the sixteenth century saw general European price levels rise 400–600% between 1500 and 1600, driven largely by the influx of American silver through Spain. This inflation redistributed wealth in predictable ways: landowners who could raise rents benefited; nobles with fixed feudal incomes were squeezed; merchants who could adjust prices thrived; wage workers who had fixed wages saw their purchasing power collapse. Some historians argue the Price Revolution helped shift economic and political power away from traditional landed nobility toward commercial bourgeoisie."
— Adapted from a history of early modern economic transformation

questions.question: The historian's argument about the Price Revolution's political effects, as described in the passage, is best understood as which of the following?

choices.text: A continuity argument demonstrating that European social hierarchies remained completely unchanged despite the silver influx
choices.explanation: The passage argues that the Price Revolution helped shift power away from traditional landed nobility toward commercial bourgeoisie — this is an argument about change in social hierarchies, not continuity.

choices.text: A description argument cataloging European price changes without explaining why they occurred or what social effects they had
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly explains why prices rose (American silver influx) and describes social effects (redistribution of wealth, power shift from nobility to bourgeoisie). This is causal explanation, not mere description.

choices.text: A causal argument linking an economic mechanism (inflation from silver influx) to a social and political outcome (shift of power from nobility to commercial classes) through differential effects on different economic actors
choices.explanation: The passage traces a causal chain: American silver → European inflation → differential effects on landowners (benefit), nobles with fixed incomes (squeezed), merchants (thrive), wage workers (hurt) → long-term shift of power toward commercial bourgeoisie. This is a causal argument linking monetary change to social-political transformation through the mechanism of differential inflation effects on different social groups.

choices.text: A comparison argument showing that European and Chinese price levels changed at identical rates due to the same silver influx
choices.explanation: The passage focuses only on European effects and does not compare European and Chinese price change rates. The argument is about European social-political transformation, not a cross-regional comparison.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"Plantation agriculture in the Americas depended on three mutually reinforcing elements: land cleared by displacing or decimating indigenous populations; labor supplied by enslaved Africans transported through the Middle Passage; and capital provided by European merchants and investors. The resulting plantation complex was not simply an agricultural system but an integrated transatlantic economic institution that connected African captives, American soil, European capital, and European consumer demand into a single productive system."
— Adapted from a history of Atlantic plantation economics

questions.question: The 'plantation complex' described in the passage most directly illustrates which of the following characteristics of early modern Atlantic commerce?

choices.text: American indigenous populations supplied the primary labor for plantation agriculture because they were accustomed to the tropical climate
choices.explanation: The passage identifies enslaved Africans as the labor supply ('labor supplied by enslaved Africans transported through the Middle Passage'). Indigenous populations had been largely displaced or decimated — they provided the land (through displacement) but not the labor.

choices.text: The plantation complex was entirely self-sufficient because plantations produced all the capital, labor, and land they needed without any external trade connections
choices.explanation: The passage describes the plantation complex as a 'transatlantic economic institution' — by definition not self-sufficient but dependent on trans-ocean connections (African slave trade for labor, European capital markets, European consumer markets). Self-sufficiency is the opposite of what is described.

choices.text: European merchant capital was the only essential component of the plantation complex, with African labor and American land being secondary and replaceable
choices.explanation: The passage presents all three elements (land, labor, capital) as 'mutually reinforcing' — they are co-essential, not hierarchically ranked with capital primary. Removing any one of the three would have made the system impossible.

choices.text: Atlantic commerce created integrated economic systems that bound together resources and people from multiple continents — African labor, American land, European capital — in ways that cannot be understood by examining any single continent in isolation
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly frames the plantation complex as an 'integrated transatlantic economic institution' connecting African captives, American soil, European capital, and consumer demand — it's definitionally a multi-continental integrated system. The argument is that you cannot understand American plantations without understanding African slave trade and European capital; all three are constitutive elements of a single system.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"When Portugal arrived at the Swahili Coast in 1498, they encountered a sophisticated network of city-states — Malindi, Mombasa, Kilwa, Sofala — connected to the Indian Ocean trade system. Portuguese attempts to dominate this trade met fierce resistance. The sultan of Mombasa repeatedly resisted and was repeatedly punished. The Portuguese established fortresses at key ports but were never able to fully control Swahili trade networks. By the late seventeenth century, Omani Arab forces allied with Swahili city-states had driven the Portuguese out of most of East Africa."
— Adapted from a history of the Portuguese East African empire

questions.question: The East African experience with Portuguese colonialism, as described in the passage, most directly illustrates which of the following?

choices.text: Portugal successfully conquered and permanently colonized all of East Africa, establishing Portuguese as the dominant language of the region
choices.explanation: The passage describes Portuguese expulsion from most of East Africa by the late seventeenth century by Omani-Swahili forces — the opposite of permanent conquest. Portuguese East African control was partial and ultimately defeated.

choices.text: Swahili city-states were economically isolated and had no connections to broader Indian Ocean trade networks before Portuguese arrival
choices.explanation: The passage describes Swahili city-states as part of a 'sophisticated network' 'connected to the Indian Ocean trade system' — they were deeply integrated into broader trade networks, not isolated. Their resistance capacity drew on these connections (including the Omani alliance).

choices.text: The Portuguese were welcomed as trading partners by all Swahili city-states because European goods were superior to anything available in the Indian Ocean trade system
choices.explanation: The passage describes fierce resistance — the sultan of Mombasa 'repeatedly resisted.' There was no universal welcome. Portuguese goods were not superior to the sophisticated goods circulating in the established Indian Ocean trade network.

choices.text: European colonial powers frequently encountered sustained resistance from established commercial and political systems that limited the depth and permanence of their control
choices.explanation: The passage shows repeated Swahili resistance (Mombasa 'repeatedly resisted'), incomplete Portuguese control of trade networks despite fortresses, and ultimate expulsion by Omani-Swahili alliance. The Swahili Coast was not passively conquered but actively contested, and European control was ultimately reversed — demonstrating that established commercial and political systems could successfully resist and reverse European colonial encroachments.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"Women in Spanish colonial Americas occupied contradictory positions. Elite creole women were expected to be confined to domestic and religious roles — convents housed thousands of high-born women who became patrons of art, authors of religious texts, and managers of substantial property. Indigenous women in encomienda territories were subject to sexual exploitation by encomenderos and labor extraction through domestic service. African enslaved women performed both agricultural labor alongside enslaved men and domestic service in colonial households. Race and class thus determined women's experience of colonial society far more than gender alone."
— Adapted from a history of women in Spanish colonial Americas

questions.question: The passage's description of women's experiences in Spanish colonial society most directly supports which of the following conclusions?

choices.text: Gender was the only social hierarchy that mattered in Spanish colonial society, with all men having power over all women regardless of race
choices.explanation: The passage argues the opposite — 'race and class determined women's experience far more than gender alone.' A poor indigenous or enslaved man had far less power than an elite creole woman. Gender alone did not determine social power.

choices.text: Colonial social hierarchies intersected with gender so that women's experiences varied dramatically based on racial and class position, making it impossible to describe a single 'female experience' in colonial settings
choices.explanation: The passage contrasts three very different female experiences (elite creole women in convents, indigenous women in encomienda territories, African enslaved women) determined by race and class within a shared colonial setting. The explicit conclusion ('race and class determined women's experience far more than gender alone') is an intersectionality argument — gender cannot be analyzed in isolation from other social hierarchies.

choices.text: Spanish colonial law prohibited all women from owning property, participating in religious life, or engaging in any economic activity
choices.explanation: The passage describes elite women as 'managers of substantial property' and 'patrons of art' — they did own property and engage in economic activities. Legal prohibition on all female economic participation is contradicted by the passage.

choices.text: All women in Spanish colonial Americas had identical social experiences regardless of racial background or class position
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly contrasts three very different female experiences divided by race and class — the claim of identical experiences is directly contradicted by the passage's entire argument.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"The Atlantic slave trade lasted approximately 400 years (1500–1900) and transported an estimated 12.5 million Africans to the Americas. Approximately 1.8 million died during the Middle Passage — the transatlantic crossing. The mortality rate on slave ships averaged 12–13%. Captives were packed into ships with minimal space, food, and water for journeys of 3–12 weeks. Disease spread rapidly in these conditions. Some enslaved people committed suicide by refusing to eat; others revolted, though only a handful of revolts succeeded in freeing enslaved people."
— Adapted from a history of the Middle Passage

questions.question: The passage's description of conditions during the Middle Passage most directly illustrates which of the following characteristics of the Atlantic slave trade?

choices.text: Slave ship captains deliberately designed comfortable conditions that minimized mortality to protect their commercial investment in enslaved people
choices.explanation: The passage describes 'minimal space, food, and water' — not comfortable conditions. The 12–13% mortality rate shows conditions were deadly, not protective of investment. Ship captains accepted mortality as a cost of maximizing cargo capacity.

choices.text: The Atlantic slave trade treated enslaved people as commodities whose lives had commercial value but whose suffering and death were acceptable costs of the trade's profitability
choices.explanation: The passage shows the logic of treating people as cargo: packed to maximize numbers (space minimized), minimum food and water (cost minimized), 12–13% mortality accepted as a commercial cost. The 1.8 million Middle Passage deaths were not accidental failures but predictable consequences of optimizing human transport for profit. Suicide and revolt are described — showing enslaved people's humanity and resistance — but the trade's logic was fundamentally commodifying.

choices.text: Enslaved Africans never resisted during the Middle Passage because the conditions of transport made organized resistance impossible
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly states 'others revolted' during the Middle Passage — resistance occurred despite conditions. The statement 'never resisted' directly contradicts the passage.

choices.text: The mortality rate on slave ships was lower than on European emigrant ships, demonstrating that slave traders cared more about human welfare than European shipping companies
choices.explanation: The passage does not compare slave ship mortality to European emigrant ships, and the described conditions (minimal space, food, water; disease spread) do not suggest greater care for welfare. Making such a comparison would require data not present in the passage.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"The question of why Portugal and Spain, not China, led the Age of Exploration has generated significant historiographical debate. China had larger ships, more advanced naval technology, and had already sent expeditions as far as East Africa (Zheng He, 1405–1433). One school of thought emphasizes political factors: Confucian bureaucrats opposed maritime trade as unproductive, and the tributary system gave China's rulers little incentive to seek new trade partners. Another emphasizes structural factors: China's internal market was so large that foreign trade was marginal to its economy, unlike Portugal, which had limited domestic market."
— Adapted from a history of early modern comparative development

questions.question: Which of the following best evaluates the two historical arguments described in the passage?

choices.text: Neither argument can explain China's maritime withdrawal because only European technological superiority can account for the difference in colonial outcomes
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly states China had 'more advanced naval technology' and 'larger ships' — European technological superiority was not the explanation. The passage presents the political and structural arguments precisely because technology cannot explain the difference.

choices.text: The political argument is certainly correct and the structural argument is certainly wrong, because political decisions always override economic incentives in historical causation
choices.explanation: 'Always override' is an overstatement — historical causation is complex and context-dependent. The passage presents both as competing scholarly arguments without resolving which is correct. Making absolute claims about causation hierarchy without evidence is not valid historical analysis.

choices.text: Both arguments attempt to explain China's maritime withdrawal by identifying factors internal to Chinese society (political culture, economic structure) rather than attributing the difference to European superiority in technology
choices.explanation: Both arguments explain the outcome through Chinese internal factors: the political argument (Confucian bureaucrats, tributary system) and the structural argument (large domestic market reducing incentive for foreign trade) both look inside China rather than attributing the difference to European technological superiority — which the passage itself says was less than China's. This is a significant interpretive choice: the passage explicitly notes China had 'more advanced naval technology,' so the explanation cannot be European technological advantage.

choices.text: Both arguments prove that China was incapable of maritime exploration due to geographic isolation from the oceans
choices.explanation: China is not geographically isolated from oceans — it has extensive coastlines on the Pacific. And the passage describes Zheng He's actual maritime expeditions to East Africa, directly contradicting claims of geographic isolation or incapability.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"The spread of Christianity to the Americas was a stated goal of European colonization from the beginning — the papal bull Inter Caetera (1493) authorized Spanish and Portuguese expansion specifically to bring Christianity to 'barbarous nations.' In practice, conversion was often coercive: indigenous temples were destroyed, priests were killed, and indigenous people were required to attend church. Yet the Christianity that emerged in the Americas was deeply syncretic, incorporating indigenous cosmological beliefs, rituals, and sacred sites into Christian practice."
— Adapted from a history of Christianity in the Americas

questions.question: The emergence of syncretic Christianity in the Americas, as described in the passage, most directly illustrates which of the following patterns in cross-cultural interactions?

choices.text: The Catholic Church in the Americas officially endorsed and documented syncretic practices as acceptable expressions of Christianity
choices.explanation: Catholic missionaries actively opposed syncretic practices — the 'Chinese Rites Controversy' (Jesuit accommodations) was condemned, and in the Americas, Indigenous religious practices were systematically persecuted. Syncretism emerged despite official opposition, not through official endorsement.

choices.text: Even when cultural transmission involves coercion, the receiving population actively shapes the transmitted culture by incorporating elements of pre-existing traditions, producing hybrid forms that neither party fully controlled or intended
choices.explanation: Coercive conversion attempted to eliminate indigenous religion and replace it with European Christianity — but the outcome was syncretic Christianity incorporating 'indigenous cosmological beliefs, rituals, and sacred sites.' Neither the colonial missionaries (who wanted pure conversion) nor indigenous converts (who wanted to preserve pre-Columbian religion) fully controlled the resulting hybrid. This 'neither party fully intended' synthesis is characteristic of forced cultural transmission in colonial settings.

choices.text: Coercive conversion always succeeds in completely replacing pre-existing religious beliefs with the imposed religion, leaving no trace of the original tradition
choices.explanation: The passage describes syncretic Christianity incorporating indigenous elements — the opposite of complete replacement. Pre-existing traditions were not erased but incorporated into the new syncretic form, surviving through transformation.

choices.text: Indigenous people in the Americas voluntarily embraced Christianity without coercion because they recognized it as intellectually superior to their own religious traditions
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly describes coercive conversion: 'temples were destroyed, priests were killed, and indigenous people were required to attend church.' Voluntary adoption without coercion is directly contradicted.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"The impact of European contact on the Americas was not limited to disease and conquest. European animals — horses, cattle, sheep, pigs, goats — multiplied explosively in New World environments that lacked natural predators. Cattle herds in Mesoamerica that began with a few hundred animals numbered in the millions within two generations. This 'eco-colonialism' transformed New World landscapes before many indigenous people had even encountered a European: forests were cleared by free-ranging cattle, water sources were fouled, and native plants replaced by Old World species that European animals preferred."
— Adapted from a history of ecological transformation in the Americas

questions.question: The concept of 'eco-colonialism' described in the passage most directly suggests which of the following about the Columbian Exchange?

choices.text: Indigenous Americans welcomed Old World animals and integrated them into their agricultural systems without any negative consequences
choices.explanation: The passage describes forests cleared, water sources fouled, and native plants replaced by Old World species — these are negative consequences for indigenous agricultural systems. There is no 'welcome' described; the transformation happened through uncontrolled animal multiplication.

choices.text: European colonizers carefully managed Old World animal populations in the Americas to prevent environmental damage and protect indigenous agricultural systems
choices.explanation: The passage describes explosive uncontrolled multiplication (hundreds to millions in two generations) with no mention of management. The transformation happened precisely because there was no management — animals reproduced freely in predator-free environments.

choices.text: The introduction of Old World animals to the Americas had no significant environmental effects because New World ecosystems quickly adapted to absorb the new species
choices.explanation: The passage describes catastrophic ecological transformation: forest clearing, water fouling, plant replacement. This is not quick, successful adaptation but ecosystem disruption.

choices.text: The biological and ecological dimensions of the Columbian Exchange transformed New World environments through cascading effects that preceded or accompanied human colonization, creating additional mechanisms of indigenous displacement beyond warfare and disease
choices.explanation: The passage describes cattle 'transforming landscapes before many indigenous people had even encountered a European' — ecological disruption operated independently of direct human-to-human contact. This adds an ecological mechanism to the better-known disease and warfare mechanisms: Old World animal introductions reshaped ecosystems, destroyed food sources, and disrupted water supplies, creating conditions that facilitated colonization even without intentional ecological warfare.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"By 1600, Spain had accumulated vast territorial claims in the Americas but faced a fundamental problem: it had few settlers willing to relocate to New World colonies. The solution was the encomienda system — granting conquistadors rights to indigenous labor — which created a small class of extremely wealthy colonizers dependent on indigenous workers. By contrast, English North American colonies had abundant willing European settlers who displaced rather than exploited indigenous populations. The different labor solutions produced different societies: Spanish colonies had racial mixing and racial hierarchy; English colonies had racial segregation and displacement."
— Adapted from a comparison of Spanish and English colonial models

questions.question: The comparison of Spanish and English colonial labor strategies described in the passage most directly supports which of the following conclusions?

choices.text: The demographic and economic circumstances of different colonial contexts produced structurally different societies, with lasting differences in racial composition, hierarchy, and the role of indigenous peoples
choices.explanation: The passage traces different social outcomes to different labor solutions: Spain (few settlers, needed indigenous labor) → encomienda → racial mixing and hierarchy; England (many settlers, displaced indigenous) → segregation and displacement. The structural social differences (racial hierarchy vs. racial segregation) were not random or purely ideological but followed from different demographic conditions (settler availability) and labor strategies.

choices.text: Spanish colonial society was entirely homogeneous because the casta system prevented any racial mixing between Spanish, indigenous, and African populations
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly states Spanish colonies 'had racial mixing' — the casta system categorized mixing, not prevented it. Racial mixing was widespread in Spanish colonies.

choices.text: English North American colonizers were morally superior to Spanish colonizers because they chose displacement over exploitation of indigenous labor
choices.explanation: Displacement of indigenous populations was not morally superior to labor exploitation — both involved violence, dispossession, and demographic devastation. The passage presents this as a structural-circumstantial difference, not a moral hierarchy between colonial systems.

choices.text: Spain had more European settlers than England and therefore built settler colonies while England used indigenous labor in extraction colonies
choices.explanation: The passage describes the opposite: Spain had 'few settlers willing to relocate' while English North American colonies had 'abundant willing European settlers.' Spain had fewer settlers, not more, which is why it relied on indigenous labor extraction.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"The Spanish required all colonial subjects to submit to the Requerimiento — a legal document read aloud (in Spanish, which indigenous people could not understand) before military action. The Requerimiento informed indigenous people of the authority of the Pope and Spanish Crown over all peoples, demanded submission, and warned that resistance would justify military force and enslavement. Soldiers sometimes read it to empty forests or to people asleep in villages at night. Spanish lawyers and theologians debated whether this procedure actually provided valid legal justification for conquest."
— Adapted from a history of Spanish colonial legal ideology

questions.question: The Requerimiento, as described in the passage, most directly illustrates which of the following about early modern European colonialism?

choices.text: The Spanish colonial legal system was primarily designed to protect indigenous rights and ensure fair treatment by conquistadors
choices.explanation: The Requerimiento was designed to justify military force and enslavement, not protect indigenous rights. It warned that resistance would be used to justify conquest — it was a tool of colonial legitimation, not indigenous protection.

choices.text: All European colonial powers used identical legal procedures before conquest, demonstrating a shared international standard for colonial legitimation
choices.explanation: The Requerimiento was a specifically Spanish legal instrument — other European powers (Portuguese, English, Dutch) used different legal justifications for colonial claims. There was no shared identical procedure across all colonial powers.

choices.text: European colonial powers used legal and religious frameworks to construct ideological justifications for conquest and enslavement, even when these frameworks were transparently inadequate for the purpose they claimed to serve
choices.explanation: The passage describes a legal document designed to justify conquest — but read in a language the audience could not understand, sometimes to empty forests. Spanish lawyers 'debated whether this procedure actually provided valid legal justification,' showing even contemporaries recognized its inadequacy. The Requerimiento illustrates that colonial powers needed legal/ideological cover for conquest even when that cover was transparently performative rather than genuinely meaningful.

choices.text: The Requerimiento demonstrated the Spanish commitment to obtaining genuine informed consent from indigenous peoples before undertaking military action
choices.explanation: Reading a Spanish document to people who could not understand Spanish, or to empty forests, is clearly not 'genuine informed consent.' The passage even notes soldiers read it 'to empty forests or to people asleep in villages at night' — not conditions for informed consent.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"The Middle Passage was not the end of the journey for enslaved Africans. Upon arrival in the Americas, survivors entered the 'seasoning' period — months in which approximately 25–30% of newly arrived enslaved people died from disease, overwork, and violence before they were considered 'seasoned' (adapted to plantation labor). Historians estimate that only 50% of people who were captured in Africa survived long enough to be put to work on American plantations, accounting for deaths during the capture process, holding at coastal forts, the Middle Passage, and the seasoning period."
— Adapted from a history of the Atlantic slave trade's mortality

questions.question: The mortality statistics described in the passage most directly support which of the following conclusions about the Atlantic slave trade?

choices.text: The 'seasoning' period demonstrated that slave traders genuinely tried to protect their human cargo because enslaved people were valuable commercial property
choices.explanation: The passage states 25–30% of newly arrived enslaved people died during 'seasoning' from 'disease, overwork, and violence' — this is not evidence of protection. The high seasoning mortality shows brutal conditions, not care for commercial property.

choices.text: The Middle Passage mortality rate was the primary cause of slave trade deaths, with capture and seasoning accounting for only minor additional mortality
choices.explanation: The passage describes four stages of mortality: capture, holding at coastal forts, Middle Passage, and seasoning — all contributing to the 50% overall loss. The Middle Passage (~12–13% mortality rate) was significant but was not the sole or primary stage of mortality.

choices.text: The total human cost of the Atlantic slave trade far exceeded the 12.5 million people transported, as the capture, transit, and seasoning process killed an additional large fraction of those originally enslaved
choices.explanation: The passage's 50% survival-to-plantation figure means that for every person who reached plantation labor, approximately one other person died in the capture/transit/seasoning process. So if 12.5 million arrived in the Americas, roughly another 12 million died before arrival — meaning total deaths from the Atlantic slave trade system were approximately double the number transported. The 12.5 million figure dramatically understates the system's total human cost.

choices.text: The 50% survival rate demonstrated that the Atlantic slave trade system was relatively humane compared to other forms of forced migration in world history
choices.explanation: A 50% death rate from capture to plantation labor is not evidence of relative humaneness — it represents a catastrophic mortality rate by any standard. Making comparative claims about 'relatively humane' requires comparative data not provided in the passage.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"The Haitian Revolution (1791–1804) produced the first successful large-scale slave rebellion in the Americas, creating an independent Black republic in the former French colony of Saint-Domingue. The revolution was inspired partly by Enlightenment ideas about natural rights that French colonists had invoked for their own purposes, and partly by African religious traditions (Vodou) that provided organizational networks for the rebellion. The revolution's success terrified slaveholders throughout the Americas, leading to tighter slave codes and increased repression in Cuba, Brazil, and the United States."
— Adapted from a history of the Haitian Revolution

questions.question: The Haitian Revolution, as described in the passage, most directly demonstrates which of the following patterns in the early modern and modern Atlantic world?

choices.text: The Haitian Revolution caused the immediate abolition of slavery throughout the Americas because slaveholders recognized the injustice of the system
choices.explanation: The passage describes the revolution causing 'tighter slave codes and increased repression' in Cuba, Brazil, and the United States — the opposite of immediate abolition. Slaveholders responded with more repression, not moral recognition of injustice.

choices.text: Enslaved people in the Americas were incapable of ideological reasoning and motivated solely by desperation rather than political ideas
choices.explanation: The passage describes Haitian revolutionaries drawing on both Enlightenment natural rights ideas and African religious traditions — demonstrating ideological reasoning, not just desperate action. The revolution was politically sophisticated, not merely reactive.

choices.text: Ideas legitimizing revolution and claims of natural rights could be turned against the colonial and slave systems by enslaved people, even when those ideas were originally articulated for other purposes
choices.explanation: The passage describes Enlightenment ideas about natural rights being used by French colonists for their own purposes — then 'turned' by enslaved people to justify their own liberation. This 'recycling of legitimizing ideology' is a recurring pattern: the same natural rights arguments that justified American and French revolutions could not logically exclude enslaved Africans, and Haitian revolutionaries demonstrated this. The success then triggered repression — showing how threatening this 'turning of ideas' was to slaveholders.

choices.text: Vodou and African religious traditions had no practical role in the Haitian Revolution and were purely personal spiritual practices without political significance
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly states Vodou 'provided organizational networks for the rebellion' — it had practical organizing functions, not merely personal spiritual significance. Religious networks enabled collective action in a context where other forms of organization were prohibited.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"The Portuguese developed the first systematic European slave trade in the 1440s, capturing Africans from the West African coast. From the beginning, Portuguese slave traders found it more efficient to purchase captives from African intermediaries than to conduct raids themselves. By 1500, approximately 150,000 Africans had been transported to Portugal and Portuguese-controlled islands (Madeira, São Tomé) to work on sugar plantations. This proto-Atlantic slave trade established the commercial networks and plantation model that would scale dramatically after the colonization of Brazil in the 1530s."
— Adapted from a history of the origins of the Atlantic slave trade

questions.question: The passage most directly supports which of the following conclusions about the origins of the Atlantic slave trade?

choices.text: African intermediaries were forced to participate in slave trading against their will through Portuguese military coercion
choices.explanation: The passage states Portuguese traders 'found it more efficient to purchase captives from African intermediaries' — this was a commercial arrangement Portuguese found efficient, not one imposed through military coercion. African intermediaries were willing commercial partners.

choices.text: The Atlantic slave trade was invented by Spanish colonizers in the Caribbean and had no prior history in Portuguese commerce
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly attributes the origin to Portuguese development in the 1440s — predating Spanish Caribbean colonization by 50 years. Spanish colonial demand accelerated the trade but did not invent it.

choices.text: The Atlantic slave trade developed from earlier Portuguese commercial networks and plantation experiments, with established patterns of supply, transport, and plantation labor that were then scaled to the American context
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly describes the Atlantic slave trade as scaling from an established base: Portuguese African slave trade (1440s) → Portuguese island plantations → Brazil (1530s). The commercial networks (African intermediaries), transport patterns, and plantation model were developed before American colonization and then applied at larger scale. This institutional pre-history explains why the Atlantic slave trade expanded so rapidly after American colonization began.

choices.text: The proto-Atlantic slave trade was limited to Portugal's European territory and had no presence in Africa or Portuguese island colonies
choices.explanation: The passage describes Portuguese-controlled islands (Madeira, São Tomé) as destinations for enslaved Africans, and African coastal trade as the source — the trade spanned the African coast and Atlantic islands, not just European Portugal.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"The period 1450–1750 saw the development of increasingly sophisticated financial instruments to support global trade. Bills of exchange allowed merchants to transfer value across continents without physically moving silver. Insurance markets (Lloyd's Coffee House in London) spread the risk of ocean voyages across multiple investors. The Amsterdam Exchange Bank (1609) created a standardized currency and clearing system for international transactions. Joint-stock companies allowed financing of multi-year expeditions through pooled capital. Together, these innovations created a global financial infrastructure that reduced transaction costs and enabled greater trade volumes."
— Adapted from a history of early modern financial innovation

questions.question: The financial innovations described in the passage most directly illustrate which of the following developments in early modern global commerce?

choices.text: Early modern financial innovations were irrelevant to global trade because all significant transactions required physical movement of gold and silver
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly states bills of exchange 'allowed merchants to transfer value across continents without physically moving silver' — this was precisely designed to make physical movement of precious metal unnecessary. Financial instruments reduced the need for physical metal transfer.

choices.text: The expansion of global trade was enabled not only by technological and navigational advances but by institutional innovations that reduced financial risk and transaction costs
choices.explanation: The passage lists financial innovations (bills of exchange, insurance, exchange banks, joint-stock companies) and frames them as reducing 'transaction costs' and enabling 'greater trade volumes' — showing that institutional/financial infrastructure was as important as ships or navigation. Global trade required solving not just transportation problems but also information asymmetry, risk management, and capital mobilization problems that financial innovation addressed.

choices.text: Early modern merchants avoided financial instruments and relied exclusively on direct barter because no trustworthy currency existed before 1750
choices.explanation: The passage describes numerous financial instruments (bills of exchange, insurance, exchange banks) operating before 1750 — the opposite of avoiding financial instruments. And silver coins were widely accepted currency throughout this period.

choices.text: Financial innovations in the early modern period were developed exclusively by governments rather than private merchants and trading houses
choices.explanation: The passage mentions Lloyd's Coffee House (private insurance market), Amsterdam Exchange Bank (a quasi-private institution), and joint-stock companies (private commercial ventures) — financial innovation came from both private and governmental sources, not exclusively government.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"The Dutch commercial empire operated differently from Spanish and Portuguese empires. The Dutch controlled trade routes and ports rather than large territorial units. They dominated the spice trade from the Moluccas (Indonesia) by violently eliminating local competition — the Dutch East India Company killed or expelled the entire population of Banda Island (approximately 15,000 people) in 1621 to gain monopoly control over nutmeg production. Dutch commercial empire was built on monopoly control of specific high-value commodities, not broad territorial colonization."
— Adapted from a history of Dutch commercial empire

questions.question: The contrast between Dutch and Spanish/Portuguese colonial strategies, as described in the passage, most directly illustrates which of the following?

choices.text: Dutch colonial strategy required large settler populations like Spanish colonies, making population transfer from the Netherlands essential to commercial success
choices.explanation: The passage describes Dutch control of trade routes and commodity monopolies rather than broad territorial colonization — this required relatively small numbers of armed traders and administrators, not large settler populations. Dutch commercial empire was deliberately minimalist in territorial and population commitment.

choices.text: The Dutch commercial empire was more humane than Spanish colonialism because the Dutch avoided all violence in establishing their trading networks
choices.explanation: The passage describes the Dutch killing or expelling the entire population of Banda Island — approximately 15,000 people — for commercial monopoly. Dutch commercial empire was built on extreme violence, not avoidance of it.

choices.text: Different European powers developed different colonial models based on their commercial goals and available resources, but all used violence to establish and maintain colonial advantage
choices.explanation: The passage contrasts Dutch (trade route and commodity control) with Spanish/Portuguese (territorial colonization) — different models. But both involved violence: Spanish conquest, Portuguese cartaz coercion, and Dutch massacre of the Banda Island population. The shared element across different colonial models is the use of violence to establish commercial monopoly and eliminate competition.

choices.text: The Dutch were unable to compete commercially with Spain and Portugal because they arrived in the Indian Ocean after all profitable trade routes were already claimed
choices.explanation: The passage describes the Dutch successfully dominating the spice trade from Indonesia — they were commercially successful competitors, not latecomers unable to compete. The Dutch actually displaced the Portuguese from many Asian trading positions.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"The early modern period saw three major types of coercive labor in the Americas: the encomienda (Spanish labor rights over indigenous people), the mita (Andean labor draft for mining), and chattel slavery (African enslaved people treated as property). All three were used to address labor shortages in labor-intensive colonial production. The relative use of each system was shaped by indigenous population density, the nature of production (mining vs. plantation vs. domestic), and the legal-ideological frameworks within which colonizers operated."
— Adapted from a comparison of colonial labor systems

questions.question: The passage's comparison of colonial labor systems most directly supports which of the following generalizations?

choices.text: Spanish colonizers preferred free wage labor over coercive systems but were forced by indigenous resistance to adopt encomienda and mita as second-best alternatives
choices.explanation: The passage attributes different labor systems to conditions (population density, production type, legal frameworks) — not to indigenous resistance forcing colonizers away from preferred free labor. Colonizers used coercive systems because they were profitable, not because free labor was somehow blocked.

choices.text: Coercive labor systems in colonial settings took multiple forms that were shaped by local conditions and needs, but all represented adaptations of the fundamental principle that colonizers would extract labor from subordinated populations by force
choices.explanation: The passage shows three different coercive forms (encomienda, mita, chattel slavery) shaped by different conditions (population density, production type, legal frameworks) — but all sharing the fundamental principle of forced extraction of labor. The variation in form does not change the shared coercive principle. This allows the generalization that coercive labor was the consistent principle, with form varying by context.

choices.text: Chattel slavery was the only form of coercive labor in the Americas, with indigenous people always working as free wage laborers on Spanish colonial plantations
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly describes encomienda and mita as coercive indigenous labor systems in addition to chattel slavery — indigenous people were subject to coercive labor, not free wage employment.

choices.text: Coercive labor systems were adopted only in the Americas and had no parallels in other parts of the early modern world
choices.explanation: Coercive labor systems appeared globally in the early modern period — serfdom in Europe and Russia, forced labor in Asian empires, and various forms of debt bondage across Afro-Eurasia. Limiting coercive labor to the Americas ignores global patterns.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"By 1750, global trade had been transformed from a set of regional networks into an interconnected world system. European ships carried goods between every inhabited continent. American silver financed trade across the Indian Ocean. African labor produced New World agricultural commodities. Asian manufactured goods — Chinese silk and porcelain, Indian cotton textiles — reached European consumers. The Atlantic, Indian Ocean, and Pacific had become parts of a single global commercial system for the first time in human history."
— Adapted from a synthesis history of early modern global trade

questions.question: The passage most directly supports which of the following conclusions about the period 1450–1750?

choices.text: Global trade had been fully integrated since ancient times through the Silk Roads and Indian Ocean networks, making the period 1450–1750 unremarkable in terms of commercial connectivity
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly states this global integration happened 'for the first time in human history' — contrasting with the pre-1450 period when trade networks were regional or interregional rather than truly global. The Silk Roads connected Eurasia but not the Americas.

choices.text: The transoceanic connections established between 1450 and 1750 created genuinely global commercial integration for the first time, linking previously separate regional trade networks into a single interdependent world system
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly argues this: 'the Atlantic, Indian Ocean, and Pacific had become parts of a single global commercial system for the first time in human history.' This is the defining claim of the passage — that 1750 represented a qualitative threshold of global integration that had not previously existed.

choices.text: European countries were the only participants in the global trade system because African and Asian peoples were excluded from commercial exchange
choices.explanation: The passage describes African labor (producing New World commodities) and Asian manufactured goods (Chinese silk, Indian cotton textiles) as integral parts of the global system — African and Asian peoples were essential participants, not excluded.

choices.text: The integration of global trade benefited all regions equally, with uniform gains distributed across every participating society
choices.explanation: The passage describes integration structurally (what was connected) without claiming equal distribution of benefits. Other passages in this unit have documented how the same global trade system involved African enslavement, indigenous population collapse, and colonial extraction — the integration was profoundly unequal in its effects.
